Mandates jurisdictions with 10,000+ residents create formal data center management plans by 2027. Includes $1,000/MW payment into EmPOWER program for low-income assistance. HB 1411 did not pass the 2026 Maryland session (sine die April 13, 2026). The community's stated objective was to mandate public environmental reporting from data centers (HB 1411).

Objective: Reform data center property tax rules in MD
Sponsors: Del. Julie Palakovich Carr (D-MD); Sen. Karen Lewis Young (D-MD)
Summary: Maryland HB1595 authorizes Baltimore City and county governing bodies to establish a special personal property tax subclass for qualified data centers and set a rate up to 2.5x the standard real property tax rate. The bill emerged from Frederick County's push for local taxing authority over data center equipment after the county discovered data centers' vast server farms were taxed at the same rate as other business personal property. The legislation was introduced February 15, 2026 and referred to the Ways and Means Committee. VERIFIED 2026-05-26: HB 1595/SB 427 (local property-tax authority over data centers) did not pass; MD sine die Apr 13, 2026.

Location: Frederick County (Pippy state moratorium bill), MD
Objective: Pause data center permits for statewide study in MD
Opposition Groups: Sugarloaf Alliance; Envision Frederick County; Frederick County Data Center Referendum Committee
Summary: Frederick County Delegate Jesse Pippy introduced a bill proposing a data center moratorium specifically for Frederick County while the state conducts a comprehensive study of data center impacts. The legislation responds to growing community concern after the Frederick County Council voted 5-2 to adopt a 2,615-acre 'Critical Digital Infrastructure' overlay zone, which prompted residents to launch a referendum petition campaign that gathered over 22,000 signatures. Maryland counties more broadly have been calling on the data center industry to slow down. NOTE: fcdcreferendum.org does NOT display a live signature count; the 22,169 figure was removed. The 21,029 figure was the Board of Elections certified count at ballot qualification (CCAN source). Signature goal was 18,000 by March 15 per the committee's own site. VERIFIED 2026-05-26: Pippy's state moratorium bill died at MD sine die Apr 13, but the citizen referendum on the data center overlay zone was certified for the Nov 2026 ballot Apr 3 with 21,029 valid signatures (now facing legal challenges).

Objective: Criticize PA governor's voluntary data center permitting program as lacking enforcement
Opposition Groups: FracTracker Alliance; Concerned Citizens of Montour County
Summary: In Pennsylvania, voluntary executive program offering expedited state permitting for data center developers who commit to bringing/paying for their own power generation, transparency and community engagement, Hiring local workers, and environmental protection including water conservation. Critics note standards are voluntary with no enforcement.

Objective: Oppose City of Industry zoning amendments enabling data center conversion of Puente Hills Mall
Opposition Groups: Avocado Heights/Industry Hills Community Alliance
Summary: City of Industry's Planning Commission voted in December 2025 to recommend zoning changes allowing data centers citywide (proposed by EdgeCore) (62 acres). City Manager Joshua Nelson wrote in May 2025 that the city was 'working on a change to our code to pretty much allow data centers anywhere.' With fewer than 300 residents, the city faced minimal public opposition. Majestic Realty pitched the Industry Business Center campus for mid-sized (20-80 MW) data centers. A formal proposal for IBC Building 10 was submitted in late 2025.

Objective: Rally against data center in City of Oakley
Opposition Groups: Oakley Community Alliance
Summary: In City of Oakley, California, Oakley City Council approved the 164-acre Bridgehead Industrial Project on March 11, 2026 in a 4-1 vote, but with an amendment excluding data centers after sustained community opposition. Residents raised concerns about water and electricity demands at public hearings and Planning Commission meetings. On February 9, 2026, the developer (Oxfoot Oakley) agreed to remove data centers from the proposal. The project was approved as a logistics/industrial hub without data center uses.

Objective: Impose 45-day data centers moratorium in City of El Monte
Opposition Groups: El Monte Neighbors United
Summary: In City of El Monte, California, On March 12, 2026, the City Council postponed action on data center moratorium evaluation after a verbal altercation during public comment led police to evacuate the chamber. Debate rescheduled to March 18, 2026. City Council unanimously passed a 45-day moratorium on March 19, 2026. Opposition was led by El Monte Neighbors United. Key concerns driving this action include community impacts. The community successfully achieved its objective.

Objective: Shield residential ratepayers from data center costs in CA
Summary: SB 978, the Data Center Community Accountability Act, introduced by Sen. Sasha Renée Pérez (D-Pasadena) on February 4, 2026. Bans new diesel backup generators at data centers in high-pollution zones, mandating clean technologies (hydrogen fuel cells or long-duration battery storage). Requires the PUC to establish a special rate class for large-scale energy users (75+ MW) to prevent cost shifts to other ratepayers. Directs the California Energy Commission to study impacts of large data center loads on decarbonization goals. Mandates prevailing wages and local skilled workforce for hyperscale construction. VERIFIED 2026-05-26: SB 978 (diesel-generator ban, special rate class, prevailing wage) passed Senate Labor 12-4 but was held in Senate Appropriations suspense ("held under submission") May 14, 2026 — effectively dead this year. Consolidated a duplicate tracker entry.

Objective: Petition to block CalEthos data center in Imperial Valley
Opposition Groups: Imperial Valley Environmental Justice Coalition
Summary: Multiple lawsuits surround data center development in Imperial Valley. The City of Imperial sued Imperial County in December 2025 to force environmental review of a $10B data center by Imperial Valley Computer Manufacturing LLC. In February 2026, the court ruled the city's CEQA claims were insufficient. In April 2026, the developer filed defamation and civil rights lawsuits against the City of Imperial and environmental activists. On April 2, 2026, a judge denied Imperial's ex parte application to block a county board vote on a lot merger. A case management conference is set for June 2, 2026. Separately, CalEthos is developing a 330 MW, 315-acre project near Niland/Calipatria. CalEthos developments (2026): launched subsidiary TerraVolt Infrastructure Inc. as a behind-the-meter 'Infrastructure-as-a-Service' platform; secured a $15M insider loan (rolled to ~$16M); and on May 11 executed a 55,000 MMBTU/day natural-gas supply agreement — but for a campus in Southeast Idaho, signaling a pivot from the original '100% geothermal/solar Lithium Valley' pitch toward natural gas and out-of-state sites (the Calipatria/Niland 315-acre campus, first phase ~420 MW, still in earlier filings). Litigation update: on April 1, 2026 IVCM/Rucci filed a defamation suit (Imperial County Superior Court) against activist Jake Tison, Comité Cívico del Valle, and Luis Olmedo — calling the nonprofit a 'greenmail extortion racket' and seeking $1M+ plus punitive damages; no anti-SLAPP ruling yet.
Sources: https://www.thedesertreview.com/business/data-center-company-to-build-315-acre-center-in-lithium-valley/article_aff75860-4f82-11ef-a8b5-7f739e431385.html; https://www.kpbs.org/news/environment/2025/12/12/imperial-valley-city-sues-to-force-environmental-review-of-massive-data-center-project; https://calexicochronicle.com/2026/01/07/where-imperial-data-center-project-and-opposition-stands/; https://calexicochronicle.com/2026/04/02/judge-denies-imperials-ex-parte-application-county-board-vote-on-data-center-lot-merger-to-continue-as-scheduled/; {'url': 'https://www.datacenterdynamics.com/en/news/calethos-launches-geothermal-subsidiary-targeting-behind-the-meter-power-for-data-centers/', 'title': 'CalEthos launches behind-the-meter subsidiary'}; {'url': 'https://sqxalts.com/calethos-secures-15m-loan-to-advance-data-center-power-plant-project/', 'title': 'CalEthos secures $15M loan'}; {'url': 'https://www.thedesertreview.com/news/developer-of-imperial-valley-ai-data-center-sues-activist-and-environmental-nonprofit-for-defamation-alleged/article_d9ca8730-b133-401b-a605-94bf8a61f702.html', 'title': 'Data center developer sues activist and nonprofit for defamation'}

Objective: Challenge Amazon/AWS water withdrawal in court
Opposition Groups: Morrow County Water Guardians; WaterWatch of Oregon
Summary: Federal class action lawsuit (Pearson v. Port of Morrow) against Amazon, Port of Morrow and 17 total defendants over nitrate contamination of 634+ domestic drinking water wells in Morrow County, where Amazon operates 13 data centers. On March 31, 2026, Amazon agreed to pay $20.5 million to settle, becoming the first Big Tech company to commit to paying damages related to public health threats from its data centers. Amazon denied any wrongdoing. The settlement funds will support private well and public infrastructure projects. Litigation continues against the remaining defendants including Port of Morrow, Lamb Weston, Madison Ranches, Threemile Canyon Farms, PGE, and Columbia River Processing.

Objective: Establish seven-member Data Center Advisory Committee to develop policy recommendations in Oregon
Summary: Governor Tina Kotek formally announced the Data Center Advisory Committee on January 20, 2026, with seven members co-chaired by Margaret Hoffmann (NW Power and Conservation Council) and Michael Jung. The committee includes Umatilla County commissioner Dan Dorran, U of O professor Greg Dotson, and energy/business professionals. It will hold at least one public meeting per month for six months, with policy recommendations due to the Governor and Legislature by October 2026. Focus areas include regulations for responsible siting, energy and water supply impacts, and protecting low-income ratepayers.

Objective: Order PJM to develop data center colocation rules at power plants
Summary: FERC PJM Co-Location proceeding (Docket EL25-49): April 17, 2026 was the deadline for reply briefs to responsive briefs in the paper hearing FERC ordered in December 2025 to determine just and reasonable rates, terms, and conditions for new transmission services serving co-located data center loads in PJM. The proceeding will set the framework for how data centers can plug directly into power plants in the 13-state PJM region serving 67 million Americans. With the record now closed, FERC is awaiting a substantive order on whether to allow expedited co-location arrangements that bypass standard transmission service.

Objective: Require data centers to report energy consumption in NJ
Summary: In New Jersey, Gov. Mikie Sherrill signed on her first day in office, Declaring a state of emergency on utility costs. Directs BPU to require electric utilities to report on energy requests from data centers ('ghost loads'). Identifies data center demand surge as key driver of NJ's energy crisis. Key concerns driving this action include tax incentive policy and water consumption. The outcome remains pending.

Objective: Study data center impacts on water resources in TX
Opposition Groups: Public Citizen Texas
Summary: Public Utility Commission of Texas conducting a survey of water use by data centers and virtual currency mining facilities. Authorized by state budget rider from Rep. Armando Walle. Survey asks about direct water use, cooling technology, and indirect water use. Results shared with Texas Water Development Board and TCEQ, due to legislature by end of 2026. Texas has 464 data centers with 70+ under development.

Objective: Challenge protective order shielding NorthWestern data center deals
Opposition Groups: Earthjustice; Montana Environmental Information Center
Summary: Earthjustice and a coalition of 9 groups challenged the Montana PSC's protective order shielding NorthWestern Energy's data center plans (letters of intent for up to 1,400 MW to 3 developers: Atlas Power, Sabey, Quantica). On March 31, 2026, the PSC ruled NorthWestern doesn't have to turn over additional data center information in its merger case, a setback for transparency advocates. Groups argue Montana's Constitution guarantees the public right to know about utility plans affecting ratepayers.

Objective: Restrict Eneus Energy 1785-acre data center in Harlingen
Opposition Groups: Harlingen residents
Summary: Harlingen city commissioners on April 1, 2026 called for public hearings and signaled plans to impose a 120-day moratorium on data centers to study impact on residents, resources and environment — but the moratorium has NOT yet been adopted. Texas law requires 30-day notice and two public hearings before imposition. Context: the Harlingen WaterWorks board in Sept 2025 entered an agreement with RGV Property Group LLC (Overland Park, KS) to potentially supply up to 4.6 million gallons/day of effluent water at 75 cents per 1,000 gallons ($1.2M/year threshold) for a proposed Eneus Energy data center project (1,785 acres on Cameron County land near Valley International Airport). Mayor Norma Sepulveda + Commissioner Daniel Lopez + City Attorney Mark Sossi drafting regulatory ordinance. [Validation 2026-06-18: status updated to 'moratorium adopted' per https://www.krgv.com/news/harlingen-officials-approve-120-day-moratorium-to-study-impact-of-potential-data-centers/.] [Validation 2026-06-18: outcome resolved to 'win'.]

Issue Category: contract_guarantees; design_standards; noise; ratepayer; water; zoning
Objective: Adopt comprehensive data center regulations with 1,000-foot residential buffers and open-loop cooling ban in New Castle County
Opposition Groups: DE-CLEAR; Delaware Sierra Club
Summary: New Castle County Council passed 12-0 (with one absent) first-of-their-kind data center regulations after 7 months of debate. Requires 1,000-foot buffer from residential zones (reducible to 500 ft with noise study), bans open-loop water cooling (unless using reclaimed water). Notably exempts projects already in the development pipeline, including Starwood's Project Washington. Effective March 19, 2026.

Objective: Require PSC operating certificate for 30MW+ facilities in Delaware
Opposition Groups: DE-CLEAR; Delaware Sierra Club
Summary: SB 205, sponsored by Sen. Hansen, requires businesses using 100+ MW of electricity (originally 30 MW, amended after industry pushback) to obtain a certificate to operate from the Delaware Public Service Commission. Certificates could mandate grid modernization payments or additional energy generation commitments. The bill remains tabled as Sen. Hansen works through business community concerns and assesses whether sufficient support exists. Delaware could see five new data centers that would double the state's total electricity usage. VERIFIED 2026-05-26: SB 205 was reported out of committee March 11 and its substance combined into HS 1 for HB 233 (May 21, 2026); no longer stalled.

Objective: Oppose QTS Data Centers data center over water in East Windsor
Opposition Groups: Twin Rivers Community (ad hoc)
Summary: QTS Data Centers is undertaking a multi-phase expansion of its 194-acre East Windsor, New Jersey, campus on Princeton Hightstown Road, formerly occupied by McGraw-Hill, with plans for 554,000 square feet of modernized facilities. Residents from the Twin Rivers community packed Planning Board hearings to challenge the expansion, raising concerns about water consumption, energy grid strain, and environmental risks from the facility's growing footprint.

Objective: Challenge TeraWulf for treaty rights violations
Opposition Groups: Sustainable Finger Lakes; No Data Center FLX; Seneca County Board of Supervisors; CLEAN (Cayuga Lake Environmental Action Now); FLX Strong; Tompkins County Legislature
Summary: The Seneca County Board of Supervisors voted unanimously in March 2026 to join Tompkins County in asking the New York DEC to reject TeraWulf's modified water permit for its proposed 400MW AI data center at a former coal plant site on Cayuga Lake in Lansing (183 acres). TeraWulf seeks to withdraw over one million gallons of water per day for cooling, with returned water at elevated temperatures that critics say would encourage harmful algal blooms threatening drinking water and tourism. Local groups have also filed a lawsuit against TeraWulf and the Lansing zoning board. Direct tenant is Fluidstack; Google holds ~8% equity in TeraWulf and backstops ~$1.8B of Fluidstack's lease — indirect link. [Validation 2026-06-18: outcome resolved to 'loss' per https://www.cornellsun.com/article/2026/04/terawulf-affiliate-approved-to-draw-1-million-gallons-per-day-from-cayuga-lake.]

Objective: Propose data centers moratorium in Town of Apex
Opposition Groups: Protect Wake County Coalition
Summary: The Apex Town Council voted unanimously on April 14, 2026 to adopt a one-year moratorium on data center development, following the March 5 withdrawal of Natelli Investments' New Hill Digital Campus (300MW, 189 acres) and sustained pressure from the Protect Wake County Coalition. The moratorium gives the town time to study impacts and develop regulations. UPDATE 2026-04-21: Apex Town Council unanimously approved the data center moratorium on April 21, 2026.

Objective: Rally against Tract data center in Hanover County
Opposition Groups: Stop Hanover Data Centers; Hanover County residents; Route 33 corridor residents; Stop Hanover Data Center VA
Summary: In Hanover County, Virginia, Denver-based developer Tract proposed the Mountain Road Technology Park — a 430-acre, 900-MW data center campus along Route 33 and Mountain Road near the Hanover-Henrico line. The project would include five buildings, requiring up to 600,000 gallons/day of water. After two deferrals in February and March, on April 16, 2026 the Hanover County Planning Commission voted 8-1 to recommend APPROVAL of the rezoning to the Board of Supervisors, despite strong community opposition (Stop Hanover Data Centers, Route 33 corridor residents). The Board of Supervisors now considers the final vote. UPDATE 2026-04-21: Planning Commission voted 8-1 (not 8-1 as previously reported) on April 16, 2026 to recommend approval of the rezoning. Board of Supervisors vote expected in May. Project details: 430 acres, Tract developer, 5 buildings, 900 MW, ~600,000 gal/day average water use (peak 2M gal/day), with $15M contribution toward booster pump infrastructure. [Update 2026-06-22: On May 28, 2026, the Hanover County (VA) Board of Supervisors voted 4-3 to DENY Denver-based developer Tract's rezoning request for the ~430-acre, 900-MW 'Mountain Road Technology Park' (13074 Mountain Road / 12517 Winns Church Road, near the Hanover-Henrico line) — overriding the Planning Commission's April 16 8-1 recommendation to approve. The campus...]
Sources: https://www.wtvr.com/news/local-news/route-33-data-center-plan-feb-19-2026; https://richmondbizsense.com/2026/02/20/hanover-planning-commission-defers-vote-on-430-acre-data-center-project/; https://www.12onyourside.com/2026/02/05/community-meeting-draws-critics-mountain-road-data-center-plan/; https://richmondbizsense.com/2025/10/30/data-center-developer-tract-proposes-mountain-road-tech-park-in-western-hanover-county/; {'url': 'https://richmondbizsense.com/2026/05/28/hanover-supervisors-reject-data-center-project-on-mountain-road/', 'title': 'Hanover supervisors reject data center project on Mountain Road (Richmond BizSense)'}; {'url': 'https://www.datacenterdynamics.com/en/news/tracts-430-acre-data-center-rejected-by-local-officials-in-hanover-county-virginia/', 'title': "Tract's 430-acre data center rejected by local officials in Hanover County, Virginia (DCD)"}; {'url': 'https://www.vpm.org/news/2026-06-01/hanover-mountain-road-data-center-tract-davis-prichard-kirchner-dibble', 'title': 'Hanover supervisors nix Mountain Road data center project (VPM)'}

Objective: Oppose Red Post Energy Group data center in Wise County
Opposition Groups: Appalachian Voices
Summary: In Wise County, Virginia, Red Post Energy Group signed a letter of intent with Wise, Virginia, Innovation Hub Venture (OASIS) to develop the Maverick Project — a phased natural gas power plant for a data center complex adjacent to Lonesome Pine Regional Industrial Facility (300 acres). The project comprises nine buildings totaling 2 million sq ft with 600 MW capacity and an on-site gas power plant. Appalachian Voices opposes the project. The complex would create 500-700 construction jobs and 30-50 operations jobs per building.

Objective: Pause data centers in Moore County pending regulations
Summary: The Moore County, Tennessee, Metro Council is considering a two-year moratorium on data center construction and expansion, which would take effect April 20, 2026. During the moratorium, the county would stop accepting new applications for special use permits, zoning amendments, and building permits related to data centers. County staff would study potential effects on energy use, water demand, noise, and land use in coordination with local utility providers.

Objective: Challenge Fundamental Data air quality permits in court
Opposition Groups: Tucker United; West Virginia Highlands Conservancy; Sierra Club
Summary: Three citizen groups -- Tucker United, West Virginia Highlands Conservancy, and the Sierra Club -- filed an appeal challenging the state Air Quality Board's decision to uphold an air quality permit for the proposed Ridgeline power plant and data center in Tucker County, located between Davis and Thomas (proposed by Fundamental Data LLC). The 500-acre facility would include a natural gas power plant fueling a data center. The appeal argues the WVDEP wrongly classified the facility as a 'synthetic minor source' rather than a 'major source' of emissions.

Objective: Shield residential ratepayers from data center costs in VT
Summary: Vermont H.727, the Sustainable Data Centers Act, sponsored by Rep. Laura Sibilia (I-Dover), regulates 'factory-sized developments' requiring 20+ MW power capacity. Focuses on proper siting, environmental protections, and ratepayer cost controls but applies the same requirements to all data centers regardless of technology or environmental footprint. A companion Senate bill, S.205, would ban data centers over 100 MW until July 2030 pending a Public Utility Commission study. VERIFIED 2026-05-26: H.727 (Sustainable Data Centers Act — siting, environmental protections, ratepayer cost controls for 20+ MW facilities) passed both chambers and was delivered to Gov. Scott on May 22, 2026; awaiting signature/veto as of May 26. On May 28, 2026, Gov. Phil Scott vetoed H.727; the next day (May 29) the House override attempt failed 83-52, short of two-thirds, killing the bill.
Sources: https://legislature.vermont.gov/bill/status/2026/H.727; https://vtdigger.org/2026/02/24/vermont-plugs-into-the-data-center-conversation/; {'url': 'https://www.environmentenergyleader.com/stories/vermont-data-center-bill-reaches-senate-third-reading,126525', 'title': 'VT data center bill reaches Senate third reading'}; {'url': 'https://governor.vermont.gov/press-release/action-taken-governor-phil-scott-legislation-may-28-2026', 'title': 'Action taken by Governor Phil Scott on legislation, May 28, 2026'}; {'url': 'https://vermontbiz.com/news/2026/may/28/scott-vetoes-data-center-bill-h727', 'title': 'Scott vetoes data center bill H.727'}

Objective: Require data centers to fund electrical infrastructure upgrades rather than shift costs to ratepayers in RI
Summary: Rhode Island S2776 requires data centers needing large amounts of electricity to fund the cost of necessary infrastructure improvements to supply that power, rather than passing costs to existing ratepayers. Rhode Island currently hosts seven data centers, none of which would meet the proposed threshold. Senate Commerce Committee recommended held for further study on March 10, 2026, alongside companion tax incentive bill S2346.

Objective: Exclude data centers from Maine BETE and Dirigo tax incentive programs (signed 2026-04-24)
Opposition Groups: Natural Resources Council of Maine
Summary: Governor Janet Mills SIGNED LD 713 on April 24, 2026, prohibiting data center projects from Maine's business development tax incentive programs. The law bars data centers from eligibility under the state's Business Equipment Tax Exemption (BETE) for facilities beginning operations on or after July 1, 2026, and excludes data centers from the Dirigo business incentives program. Maine's Department of Economic and Community Development must study other potential financial incentives for data centers and report by November 4, 2026. Mills signed LD 713 on the same day she vetoed LD 307.

Objective: Oppose tax incentives for Sentinel Data Centers in Jay
Summary: Sentinel Data Centers has an agreement to occupy 1 million square feet on two floors of a former paper mill in Jay, Maine, employing 800-1,000 construction workers and 125 permanent staff. The project faces an existential threat from LD 307, a bill advancing in the Maine Legislature that would impose a two-year moratorium on data centers above 20MW. Notably, the developer has not encountered local opposition from Jay residents, partly because the project does not seek special tax breaks.

Objective: Oppose Northern New England Energy data center over noise in Sanford
Opposition Groups: Sanford Residents Concerned About Data Centers
Summary: Northern New England Energy Company is proposing the 'Sanford Woods Industrial and Technical Campus,' a 100-300MW data center covering over 1,000 acres along the Mousam River in Sanford. The developer claims the facility would generate its own power using fuel cells, solar, and battery storage without burdening Maine's grid. The project is threatened by LD 307, a proposed state moratorium on data centers above 20MW. Local residents have raised concerns about noise, environmental damage, and the significant changes a facility of this scale would bring. [Validation 2026-06-18: status updated to 'moratorium' per https://www.pressherald.com/2026/05/19/sanford-imposes-emergency-data-center-moratorium-halting-mousam-river-project/.]

Objective: Codify bring-your-own-power mandate for data centers in Connecticut
Sponsors: Joint Committee on Energy and Technology
Summary: After the NE Edge/Millstone nuclear data center project was scrapped, Governor Lamont signaled Connecticut may require new data centers to generate their own power. The state's high electricity prices have made it less attractive for data centers. Only one company has used CT's data center tax incentive since 2021. No moratorium bills introduced. VERIFIED 2026-05-26: The "bring your own power" concept lived in HB 5469, which failed to advance before CT sine die May 6, 2026; Gov. Lamont's policy signal remains but no bill was enacted.

Objective: Oppose $10B data center backed by Lt. Gov. Jones over water strain in Butts County
Opposition Groups: Altamaha Riverkeeper; Georgians for Integrity
Summary: A massive estimated $10 billion data center project backed by Georgia Lt. Gov. Burt Jones and his family is proposed for Butts County as part of the Highway 16 Interstate Health Development near I-75, which would include 11 million square feet of data centers. The project is expected to consume over 4.5 million gallons of water per day, more than tripling Butts County's current water usage. Georgia Attorney General Chris Carr accused Jones of using his office to benefit his family's property interests, and environmental advocates have flagged the lack of available information about the facility's cooling technology and energy demands. [Validation 2026-06-19: outcome resolved to 'loss' per https://www.jacksonprogress-argus.com/news/commissioners-rezone-560-acres-off-ga-highway-16-and-i-75/article_6c68e37a-8835-435c-a033-3728a6ccbc07.html.]

Objective: Limit data center tax abatements to 20 years and require 100MW+ sales tax payments (HB 399)
Summary: Alabama HB 399, sponsored by Rep. Hulsey (R-Helena), is the House companion to SB 265. Limits tax abatements for data center projects over $200M to 20 years, down from the current 30-year maximum. Requires data centers consuming 100+ MW to pay state sales and use taxes allocated to the General Fund, effective January 1, 2027. Passed House 103-0; Senate unanimously passed amended version (April 9, 2026); House concurred and bill was sent to Gov. Ivey's desk. Gov. Ivey publicly supports the legislation as part of a package to lower costs for families.

Objective: Impose one-year moratorium on large-scale solar facilities tied to data center expansion in Alabama
Opposition Groups: Stockton Area Residents Against Solar
Summary: In Alabama, SB 354 would impose a one-year moratorium on construction of new large-scale solar power facilities, Prompted by opposition to a 4,500-acre solar project in Stockton meant to power Meta's Montgomery data center expansion. Exempts TVA solar capacity and projects already under construction. Opposition was led by Stockton Area Residents Against Solar. Key concerns driving this action include environmental impacts and loss of farmland. The outcome remains pending. VERIFIED 2026-05-26: SB 354 passed the Alabama Senate 27-4 but died on a procedural transmission deadline in early April. Direction is ambiguous for this tracker — it is an anti-SOLAR moratorium, but the targeted Stockton solar farm exists to power Meta's data center; outcome left unscored. [Validation 2026-06-21: status -> 'died' per https://alabamareflector.com/2026/04/08/one-year-moratorium-on-solar-farms-fails-on-alabama-senate-procedural-rule/.]
Sources: https://www.alreporter.com/2026/03/12/bill-to-pause-new-solar-developments-advances-to-alabama-senate-for-vote/; https://pv-magazine-usa.com/2026/03/16/alabama-senator-seeks-statewide-ban-on-solar-installations/; https://heatmap.news/energy/meta-solar-alabama; {'url': 'https://alabamareflector.com/2026/04/08/one-year-moratorium-on-solar-farms-fails-on-alabama-senate-procedural-rule/', 'title': 'AL solar moratorium fails on procedural rule'}

Objective: Propose data centers moratorium in Caddo Parish
Opposition Groups: Caddo Lake Institute
Summary: Following Governor Jeff Landry's announcement of Amazon's $12 billion investment in three interconnected data center campuses across Caddo, Louisiana, and Bossier parishes, Commissioner Chris Kracman proposed a one-year moratorium on developments using water from Caddo Lake, but the resolution failed to receive a second from any other commissioner. Amazon has pledged to limit cooling water use to less than 13% of the year and invest $400 million in public water systems in the Caddo-Bossier area.

Objective: Challenge STACK Infrastructure 1,089-acre data center in Bossier Parish
Opposition Groups: Benton Area Residents Concerned About Data Centers
Summary: As part of Amazon's $12 billion Northwest Louisiana data center investment, STACK Infrastructure is developing a 1,089-acre 'Highway 3 Technology Park' north of Benton in Bossier Parish, planned for over 1.3 million square feet across six buildings. The Bossier Parish Police Jury scheduled a public hearing for March 18, 2026 -- the first opportunity for public comment since the project was confirmed. Residents have raised concerns about air quality, proximity to homes, water supply, and rising utility costs. [Validation 2026-06-21: outcome -> 'loss' per https://www.ktalnews.com/news/local-news/bossier-parish-data-center-approved/.]

Objective: Create public records exemption shielding data center siting plans for 12 months (SB 1118)
Summary: Florida SB 1118 creates a public records exemption allowing local governments to keep data center siting plans confidential for up to 12 months when requested by the company. A developer's 'proprietary confidential business information' remains exempt until it becomes public or the company stops treating it as proprietary. Includes a 5-year sunset provision expiring October 2031. The bill died in committee alongside companion SB 484, which establishes a comprehensive regulatory framework for hyperscale data centers effective July 1, 2026.

Objective: Legal challenge to Monticello Tech data center in Monticello
Opposition Groups: Minnesota Center for Environmental Advocacy; Stop the Monticello Data Centers MN
Summary: UPDATE: Monticello residents rallied against two proposed data centers (600+ acres combined, 4M+ sq ft). An online petition exceeded 1,200 signatures. Minnesota Center for Environmental Advocacy filed a lawsuit against the city challenging the review process. A Facebook opposition group quickly gained traction. Residents gathered at local parks to distribute yard signs.

Objective: Oppose CloudHQ data center over noise in Chaska
Opposition Groups: Clover Ridge Neighborhood Residents
Summary: CloudHQ's 1.4M sq ft data center at West Creek Corporate Center received preliminary approval from the Chaska, Minnesota, City Council on October 21, 2024, with a 4-1 vote. The lone dissenter was Ward 2 representative Taylor Hubbard. Residents voiced concerns about the 72.5-foot building height (double the 36-foot limit), noise, and neighborhood character impacts. Planning Commission raised issues about unprecedented height and landscaping requirements. Final site plan approval is pending future Planning Commission and City Council review.

Objective: Approve conditional use permit for Crusoe 340-acre data center in Warrenton with conditions
Opposition Groups: Warrenton Residents Against Data Centers
Summary: The Warrenton, Missouri, Board of Aldermen voted unanimously in January 2026 to approve a conditional use permit for a Crusoe data center on a 340-acre parcel, consisting of two 805,000-square-foot buildings and a 40,000-square-foot administration building. More than 40 residents spoke both for and against the proposal, with opponents raising concerns about noise, light pollution, water supply impacts on aquifers, and the pace of the approval process.

Objective: Block Gateway Digital Campus rezoning of 600 acres of farmland
Opposition Groups: Franklin County Rural Coalition
Summary: Gateway Digital Campus and Provident Data Centers are seeking to rezone roughly 600 acres of agricultural land near I-44 and Robertsville Road in Villa Ridge, Missouri, for a hyperscale data center, sitting directly adjacent to the Shaw Nature Reserve. The project returned to the Franklin County Planning & Zoning Commission on March 17, 2026 despite being tabled for 6 months in January. Both Gateway and the Beltline Energy project are on the same March 17 agenda, alongside proposed county-wide data center regulations including 400ft setbacks, 800-acre site limits, and sound limits.

Objective: Oppose Alcove Development data center over water in Osawatomie
Opposition Groups: Osawatomie Residents Concerned About Data Centers
Summary: The city of Osawatomie, Kansas, entered a pre-development agreement with Alcove Development/Northland Data Centers for a $900 million to $1.1 billion data center project on 115 acres of city-owned land, requiring one million gallons of water and 150MW of power per day. Residents have pushed back, citing the city's aging water infrastructure, fears of noise, and lack of community input. The Miami County Commission is considering a temporary moratorium countywide while proper regulations are developed.

Objective: Prohibit eminent domain for data center land acquisition
Sponsors: Sen. Barry DeKay (at Gov. Pillen's request)
Summary: LB1261 was signed April 10, 2026, allowing private developers to build and own power plants to serve large-scale electricity users like data centers, and prohibiting Nebraska's public power utilities from using eminent domain to acquire such plants. Connected to Google's proposed Nebraska data center that could require 1,000-3,000 MW (3x Lincoln's peak demand). The Legislature passed it 34-7 despite opposition arguing it fast-tracks a new business model threatening Nebraska's land, water, and utility rates.

Objective: Halt data centers in Troy after Cloverleaf Infrastructure proposal
Opposition Groups: Troy Residents for Responsible Growth; Say NO to Data Centers in Troy IL
Summary: The Troy, Illinois, Planning Commission approved a six-month moratorium on data processing centers in March 2026, following the collapse of Cloverleaf Infrastructure's proposal for a 1.4-million-square-foot hyperscale data center sited less than a mile from neighborhoods, daycares, and senior living facilities. Hundreds of residents packed a meeting at Triad High School in February to challenge the project. The moratorium prevents new data center applications while the planning commission strengthens zoning ordinances. Troy Planning Commission approved a six-month moratorium on data processing centers on March 13, after Cloverleaf Infrastructure's 500MW proposal collapsed following massive public opposition. Hundreds of residents packed Triad High School in February. City Council final passage expected March 16.

Objective: Oppose HydraVault data center over water in Chicago South Loop
Opposition Groups: Prairie District Neighborhood Alliance
Summary: HydraVault received a full building permit from Chicago, Illinois, to construct a 76,000-square-foot, 20-megawatt AI data center at 2538 S. Wabash Avenue in the South Loop. Residents reported opposition due to energy consumption, water use, and the lack of public hearings — the project was classified as a 'minor change' by zoning officials, bypassing community review.

Issue Category: community_impact; contract_guarantees; environmental; noise; water; air_quality
Objective: Rally against Pioneer Development data center in Yorkville
Opposition Groups: Block Project Steel and Project Cardinal
Summary: After a nearly six-hour contentious city council meeting, Yorkville, Illinois, approved Pioneer Development's 'Project Cardinal' — a 1,037-acre, 1.8-gigawatt data center campus with 14 buildings totaling 17 million square feet, plus a $51 million upfront payment. Residents spoke against it, citing water usage, noise, air quality, and property value concerns. Yorkville City Council approved Project Cardinal at 12:51 AM on March 12 after a nearly 6-hour contentious meeting. The 1,037-acre campus (Pioneer Development LLC) includes 14 buildings totaling 17M sq ft. Development agreement includes $51M in upfront payments over 4 years and $68.25M directed to the school district ($91M total). First data center operational by 2029.

Objective: Rally against Cloverleaf Infrastructure data center in Edwardsville
Opposition Groups: Residents for Responsible Growth
Summary: Cloverleaf Infrastructure has been in detailed discussions with Edwardsville, Illinois, officials since mid-2025 about a data center south of New Poag Road. Obtained emails revealed extensive behind-the-scenes planning despite public assurances of 'no formal proposal.' Residents for Responsible Growth organized a meeting of nearly 300 people in March 2026.

Objective: Impose 12-month data centers moratorium in Sprigg Township
Opposition Groups: Food & Water Watch
Summary: Sprigg Township, Ohio, trustees passed a 12-month voluntary moratorium on data centers in late February 2026, in response to a proposed facility near the former Stuart power plant requiring 1,300 megawatts — 31 times Adams County's annual power consumption. First of two Adams County localities to act, followed by Monroe Township. Tenant under NDA; Meta not confirmed.

Objective: Impose 180-day data centers moratorium in Ashville (EdgeConneX)
Opposition Groups: Protect Pickaway Alliance
Summary: Ashville, Ohio, Village Council unanimously passed a 180-day emergency moratorium on December 20, 2025, in response to EdgeConneX's proposal on 680 acres including an on-site 200-megawatt natural gas power plant. Residents lined yards with 'No Data Center' signs. EdgeConneX withdrew its annexation application but reportedly already holds land zoned for data center use.

Objective: Ban data center NDAs in Wisconsin (original bipartisan bill)
Opposition Groups: River Alliance of Wisconsin
Summary: Senate Bill 969, authored by Sen. Andre Jacque (R-New Franken), would prohibit NDAs between data center companies and municipalities, while still allowing withholding of genuine trade secrets. The bill was prompted by a Wisconsin Watch investigation revealing at least four communities had signed secrecy deals hiding billion-dollar data center plans. Rep. Clint Moses (R-Menomonie), author of the companion Assembly bill, called the measure 'really about trust.' Passed Senate Utilities Committee 4-1, but the Senate adjourned the 2026 session without a floor vote.

Objective: Propose data centers moratorium in Tulsa
Opposition Groups: Protect Sand Springs Alliance
Summary: Tulsa City Council unanimously passed a NINE-month data center moratorium (through December 31, 2026) on March 26, 2026. Originally proposed by Councilor Laura Bellis as a 365-day moratorium, it was amended to 9 months in committee.

Objective: Pause data center permits for study via SB 1488
Opposition Groups: Oklahoma ratepayer advocates; Stop Data Colonialism; Honor the Earth
Summary: SB 1488, sponsored by Republican Sen. Sacchieri, would have imposed a three-year moratorium on data centers with electrical load greater than 100 MW until November 1, 2029. Directed the Oklahoma Corporation Commission to study impacts on water supply, utility rates, property values, and ideal siting locations. The bill missed key legislative deadlines and died without advancing from committee.

Objective: Veto borough resolution marketing public land for Terra Energy Center data center development
Opposition Groups: Susitna River Coalition
Summary: The Mat-Su Borough Assembly plan to help a private company find investors/developers for a large data center on borough-owned land was vetoed by Mayor Edna DeVries, but the Assembly voted 6-1 to override the veto on March 17, 2026. More than a dozen people testified against data centers, while about three spoke in support. Opponents cited energy costs (14-17 cents/kWh in Southcentral), tightening Cook Inlet gas supply, and environmental concerns.

Objective: Deny Franks Investment Company conditional use permit in Shreveport
Opposition Groups: West Shreveport neighborhood residents
Summary: Shreveport, Louisiana, Metropolitan Planning Commission denied a special use permit for a 2.8 million sq ft Franks Investment Company LLC data center at 7340 Greenwood Road (Resilient Technology Park) on a 4-4 vote on December 3, 2025, after major community backlash over noise, water use (7.5M gallons/day), and environmental impacts. The Shreveport City Council overturned the denial on December 18, 2025 in a unanimous 7-0 vote, and separately approved selling up to 7.5M gallons of water daily to the facility. A subsequent lawsuit was filed accusing the city of violating constitutional obligations.

Objective: Oppose Amazon/AWS data center over water in Hermiston
Summary: Hermiston, Oregon, City Council voted in September 2025 to annex 800 acres of land to attract more data centers, with only one councilor (Jackie Linton) voting against. Linton raised concerns about water supply impacts and inadequate review time. Amazon Web Services already has dozens of data centers across Umatilla and Morrow counties and is building two more in Hermiston. The annexation was approved over community objections.

Objective: Protect Oklahoma ratepayers from data center cost shifts via HB 2992
Summary: Oklahoma HB 2992, the Data Center Consumer Ratepayer Protection Act, passed the Oklahoma House 92-2 on March 23, 2026. The bill passed the Senate Energy and Telecommunications Committee 9-0 on April 16, 2026 with an amendment, and now awaits a full Senate floor vote. HB 2992 defines 'large load customers' as new facilities adding 75+ MW and sets guidelines for how electric suppliers address rising energy demands from data centers, crypto mining, and AI facilities. It aims to shield residential and small business customers from higher utility bills tied to data center infrastructure.

Objective: Ban data center water withdrawals over 2M gallons/day (SB 761-763)
Sponsors: Sen. Rosemary Bayer (D-MI); Sen. Sue Shink (D-MI); Sen. Erika Geiss (D-MI)
Opposition Groups: Sierra Club Michigan Chapter
Summary: Michigan Senate Bills 761, 762, and 763 (Sens. Rosemary Bayer D-West Bloomfield, Sue Shink D-Northfield Township, Erika Geiss D-Taylor) introduced December 2025. SB 761 bans data centers withdrawing >2 million gallons/day for consumptive use (water that evaporates or doesn't return to systems). SB 762 requires the Michigan PSC to publish annual reports on total energy spending and water usage of data centers. SB 763 prohibits passing infrastructure improvement costs on to residential ratepayers. As of March 2026, none of the bills have passed out of committee. Separately, Reps. Wortz, Fox, and Wegela introduced a bipartisan three-bill House package (the 'Data Center Regulation Act') that would prohibit new data centers from receiving permits or commencing operations until April 1, 2027. Speaker Matt Hall and Gov. Whitmer have not commented, raising doubts about passage.

Objective: Establish right to compute, limiting local data center regulation
Summary: The Montana Right to Compute Act (SB 212), signed by Governor Gianforte on April 16, 2025, establishes a legal right to own and use computational resources including data centers. The law requires any government regulation restricting computing resources to be 'demonstrably necessary and narrowly tailored,' effectively limiting local governments' ability to restrict data center siting or operations through zoning. The law was enacted without significant organized opposition, representing a setback for community control advocates.

Objective: Create data center tax incentive program in MS
Summary: Mississippi SB 3168, enacted July 1, 2025, expands the state's data center tax incentive program. The law grants sales and use tax exemptions to data centers investing $250M+ and creating 35+ jobs at 125% of average state wages, adds electricity purchases to exempt 'state taxes,' provides automatic 10-year extension eligibility for qualifying expansions ($100M+), and excludes cryptocurrency mining. The bill passed both chambers and was signed into law without significant opposition.

Objective: Fast-track federal permitting for $500M+ data center and energy projects via EO 14318
Summary: Executive Order 14318, 'Accelerating Federal Permitting of Data Center Infrastructure,' was signed by President Trump on July 23, 2025. The order directs agencies to streamline NEPA reviews, establish new categorical exclusions for data center and energy projects, and designate qualifying projects ($500M+ capital or 100+ MW load) as 'transparency projects' for expedited FAST-41 review. It authorizes use of federal land for data centers, with DOE selecting four initial sites, and directs the Commerce Department to provide financial support including loans, grants, and tax incentives. The order was enacted without Congressional action.

Objective: Mandate transparency and reporting from data centers in VA
Opposition Groups: Virginia Data Center Reform Coalition; Piedmont Environmental Council
Summary: Virginia HB 784 provides additional transparency by requiring an annual report disclosing information about each data center operator that receives a sales tax exemption, including whether they've met job and investment goals. Part of Virginia's massive 2026 legislative push: lawmakers considered 61 data center bills, sent 15 to the governor's desk, and carried over 46 to 2027. As of late March 2026, the data center tax exemption (~$1.6B/year) remains unresolved as the Senate seeks to eliminate it entirely while the House wants to tie it to environmental compliance. This budget standoff has delayed the state budget; a special session is set for April 23 to resolve the issue. Among bills passed: HB 507 (Tier IV generators required for new data centers, awaiting governor) and HB 1393 (rate-recovery for new generation costs from large electric demand customers). [Validation 2026-06-18: status updated to 'incorporated' per https://legiscan.com/VA/text/HB784/2026.]

Objective: Impose $3/sq ft tax on data centers above 25,000 sq ft for conservation funding
Sponsors: Sen. Russet Perry (D-VA); Sen. Richard Stuart (R-VA)
Opposition Groups: Virginia Data Center Reform Coalition; Piedmont Environmental Council
Summary: Virginia SB 393, the Virginia's Great Outdoors Act (companion HB 641), was defeated in February 2026. The bill would have created a $250M/year dedicated funding stream for land conservation, public lands, trails, and outdoor recreation funded by a $3/sq ft annual tax on data centers above 25,000 sq ft. The Senate Finance Resources Subcommittee voted to 'carry over' SB 393, delaying it indefinitely. Both HB 641 and SB 393 have been defeated. The proposal would have established Virginia's largest-ever conservation fund, including a Virginia Tribal Commitment Fund, and was supported by PEC, NVCT, and conservation groups statewide.

Objective: Prohibit utility cost recovery for 100MW+ data center transmission from residential ratepayers
Opposition Groups: Piedmont Environmental Council; Virginia Data Center Reform Coalition
Summary: Prohibits Virginia utilities from recovering costs of transmission lines and energy generation serving data centers over 100 MW from residential ratepayers. Aimed at addressing the $4.4 billion in data center transmission costs borne by customers across seven PJM states in 2024 alone. House subcommittee recommended continuing to next session. Part of a broader package of 2026 Virginia data center bills targeting ratepayer protections and industry accountability.

Objective: Require labor peace agreements for data center tax credits
Summary: Illinois SB 2182, sponsored by Sen. Elgie Sims Jr. (D), would require data centers receiving state tax credits to enter into labor peace agreements with unions representing workers who operate and maintain critical systems. Under LPAs, employers agree not to interfere with unionization efforts while unions agree not to strike. The bill would add labor requirements to Illinois's existing Data Center Investment Program and is advancing through the Senate.

Objective: Uphold denial of Centra Logistics data center on appeal
Opposition Groups: New Market Village HOA; New Market Village residents
Summary: In Virginia, Henrico County's Board of Zoning Appeals unanimously denied Centra Logistics' vested rights claim for a data center complex on nearly 200 acres along Darbytown Road in the Varina district on Feb. 26, 2026. CEO David Wagner had filed the claim in October 2025, Arguing the company had a right to build under the pre-June 2025 rules. But a key factor was that Wagner did not purchase the site until November 2025 -- a month after filing his claim. The company had previously withdrawn its provisional use permit application. Henrico adopted new rules in June 2025 requiring all hyperscale data centers to go through public hearings. Centra may appeal to Henrico Circuit Court.

Objective: Require use permit for WestDulles Properties data center in Chesterfield County
Opposition Groups: Virginia Data Center Reform Coalition
Summary: Reston-based developer WestDulles Properties filed an appeal in March 2026 of Chesterfield County staff's determination that a Conditional Use Permit (CUP) is required before its 170-acre data center development at 1500 Battery Brooke Parkway in the James River Industrial Center can be built. Chesterfield Planning Director Andy Gillies determined the project requires a CUP as of January 1, 2026 under the new zoning ordinance making data centers conditional uses in industrially zoned land. WestDulles argues no CUP is needed because the site is zoned I-3 Heavy Industrial, where data centers were a by-right use under the previous ordinance. The matter is slated to go before the Chesterfield Board of Zoning Appeals in May 2026. Mass-grading plan was received by the county in fall 2025.

Objective: Block Province Group rezoning of 181 acres in Powhatan County
Opposition Groups: Virginia Data Center Reform Coalition
Summary: Powhatan County, Virginia, Board of Supervisors voted 5-0 on Oct. 29, 2025 to approve rezoning of approximately 61 additional acres to expand the planned Province Group data center campus from 120 to 181 acres (1.5M to 2M sq ft). This came despite the Planning Commission's unanimous recommendation of denial on Oct. 7. The $2.7B campus near the Powhatan-Chesterfield line could eventually house up to four data center buildings requiring 300-400 MW of power and a new substation. Estimated annual tax revenue at full build-out: $9.7 million. The original 120-acre campus was approved 3-2 in October 2024.

Objective: Deny Amazon Web Services data center on 52 acres in Prince William
Opposition Groups: Coalition to Protect Prince William County; Virginia Data Center Reform Coalition
Summary: Amazon proposed three 110-foot-tall data center buildings totaling nearly 1.1 million sq ft on 52 acres along Ashton Avenue near Manassas Mall, sandwiched between three schools and a townhome development. The Prince William County, Virginia, Planning Commission voted 6-1 in June 2024 to recommend denial due to noise, wildlife, and neighborhood proximity concerns. The Board of County Supervisors shelved the proposal indefinitely in October 2024 and it was deferred again in January 2025 to a date uncertain. Amazon maintains it still plans to build on the site but no hearing date is set.

Location: DeKalb County (nuclear data center regulations), TN
Objective: Ban data centers in designated zones in DeKalb County
Summary: DeKalb County, Tennessee, Commission is considering data center regulations proposed by County Mayor Matt Adcock after receiving an inquiry about locating a nuclear-powered data center in the county. The proposed resolution would prohibit data centers within 5,000 feet of residences, schools, daycares, parks, churches, commercial, or industrial establishments. Applicants would need permits with appeal rights. Commissioner Tom Chandler called the resolution 'a sham' arguing the 5,000-foot restriction makes permit approval impossible anywhere in the county. The action was tabled to allow public input; no final vote recorded.

Objective: Restore local government authority over data centers in WV
Opposition Groups: West Virginia Environmental Council; West Virginia Center on Budget & Policy; West Virginia Highlands Conservancy
Summary: Would restore local government control over data center siting in West Virginia, reversing HB 2014 — the Power Generation and Consumption Act signed by Gov. Morrisey — which allows data centers to operate on independent microgrids and strips counties of jurisdiction over siting and size. Tucker County residents pushed for the bill amid growing backlash over development in their communities. Never received a committee hearing during the session that ended March 14, 2026.

Objective: Accelerate data center tax exemption sunset in VA
Opposition Groups: Virginia Data Center Reform Coalition; Piedmont Environmental Council; Good Jobs First
Summary: The Virginia Senate budget proposes accelerating the data center tax exemption sunset from 2035 to January 2027, which would redirect $1.6B/year to tax rebates, transportation, and water infrastructure. The House budget preserves the exemption but adds clean energy requirements. The Virginia Data Center Reform Coalition, Piedmont Environmental Council, and Good Jobs First are pushing for the accelerated sunset. The General Assembly reconvenes April 22 to resolve the stalemate.

Objective: Block data center rezoning in Maricopa County
Opposition Groups: Stop Project Baccara
Summary: The Maricopa County, Arizona, Board of Supervisors unanimously approved a modernized zoning ordinance establishing specific rules for data center development, effective January 9, 2026. The new rules limit data centers to two industrial zoning districts (IND-2 and IND-3), requiring full rezoning for any other land.

Objective: Mobilize opposition to CoreSite data center in Denver
Opposition Groups: Elyria-Swansea community residents; GES Coalition
Summary: In Colorado, CoreSite is building a 600,000-square-foot data center in Elyria-Swansea, One of the most polluted zip codes in the country (80216), which proceeded as a by-right use without community review. Hundreds packed a February 2026 meeting demanding transparency, and delivered a 'Good Neighbor' proposal. Denver's mayor subsequently joined calls for a moratorium.

Objective: Adopt data center zoning standards in Tucson
Opposition Groups: Tucson Clean Energy Coalition
Summary: Tucson, Arizona is advancing a Unified Development Code (UDC) amendment to regulate data centers. The proposed amendment requires a minimum 400 ft setback from residential uses, mandates a developer-hosted neighborhood meeting, eliminates by-right entitlement by requiring a Zoning Examiner public hearing, and requires demonstration of adequate water supply. Public notification is expanded to a half-mile radius (vs. current 400 ft). The amendment remains on an active agenda following the April 6 study session; city council action is expected in the coming weeks. On June 3, 2026, Tucson’s Planning Commission advanced tighter rules for data centers larger than 25,000 sq ft or 20 MW (stricter residential setbacks, noise/grid/decommissioning/habitat standards, and Planned Area Development approval instead of by-right). ~200 participated and hearings were extended, with a recommendation to mayor/council due within ~45 days; the city also launched a Large Quantity Water User (>7M gal/month) disclosure program. [Update 2026-06-22: UPDATE to Tucson's draft large-scale data center zoning regulations. The Tucson Planning Commission held a follow-up public hearing June 17, 2026 (after its June 3 session where it took no final action but voted unanimously to fold in public comments and energy updates). The proposed PAD-style rules would apply to data center projects larger than 25,000...]
Sources: https://www.kjzz.org/fronteras-desk/2026-03-30/tucson-public-has-until-end-of-month-to-weigh-in-on-proposed-amendment-for-data-centers; https://www.kjzz.org/fronteras-desk/2026-04-06/tucson-leaders-will-discuss-a-proposal-on-data-center-restrictions-this-week; https://www.tucsonsentinel.com/local/report/022026_datacenter_zoning/tucson-considers-new-zoning-rules-data-centers/; {'url': 'https://www.kjzz.org/fronteras-desk/2026-06-04/tucson-officials-move-closer-to-passing-tighter-rules-for-data-center-development', 'title': 'Tucson officials move closer to passing tighter data center rules'}; {'url': 'https://news.azpm.org/p/environmentnews/2026/6/5/230034-tucson-planning-commission-has-more-questions-about-proposed-data-center-regulations/', 'title': 'Tucson Planning Commission has more questions about proposed data center regulations — AZPM'}

Objective: Challenge three data center approvals totaling 3,900 acres in Pinal County
Opposition Groups: RAZE (Rural Arizona Engagement); No Desert Data Center Coalition
Summary: Despite community opposition from RAZE and the Ak-Chin Indian Community, Pinal County, Arizona, approved three major data center projects in November 2025 totaling ~3,900 acres, including the $33 billion Vermaland 'La Osa' project near Eloy. On April 16, 2026, the Pinal County Planning and Zoning Commission voted 7-2 to recommend approval of rezoning for the 3,385-acre La Osa Energy Center south of Eloy. The project includes up to 59 data center buildings (~400,000 sq ft each), two on-site gas-fired power plants, and battery energy storage systems. Estimated construction cost is $10 billion or more. The recommendation now moves to the Pinal County Board of Supervisors for final action.

Objective: Block Antelope Data Center on 640 acres near Cedar City over water and farmland loss
Opposition Groups: Iron County Citizens for Responsible Growth; Defending Utah
Summary: In Iron County, Utah, 350+ residents packed Festival Hall on March 5, 2026 for a hearing on the Antelope Data Center proposed for 640 acres on Antelope Springs Road, 15 miles NW of Cedar City. Of 15+ speakers over 2 hours, only one supported the project. Concerns: water (2.6M gallon initial fill), utility prices, farmland loss, noise, pollution. On June 4-5, 2026, the Iron County Planning Commission unanimously approved a conditional-use permit for the Antelope Data Center (developer Pronghorn Development LLC) — 640 acres, ~5 buildings and 300 MW — which proceeded despite the County Commission’s new 180-day moratorium because its application was already deemed "complete." No hyperscaler is named. The June 4 CUP approval was unanimous and carries 62 conditions (water rights, wastewater, roads, wildlife, noise, lighting, cultural surveys, site restoration) — more than every prior Iron County project combined. The Pronghorn 'Antelope' campus is 640 acres, up to five buildings totaling ~6.75 million sq ft with an on-site natural-gas plant up to 1.5 GW, phased over 8-10 years. It was exempt from the county's new 180-day moratorium because its application was deemed complete beforehand. Residents held up 'appeal' signs, but no formal appeal had been filed as of June 7.
Sources: https://www.stgeorgeutah.com/news/hundreds-pack-iron-county-hearing-as-residents-voice-concerns-over-proposed-ai-data-center/article_21d47585-32c8-4f77-9a2d-adc3f5e388d5.html; https://www.ksl.com/article/51463149/planning-commission-considers-ai-data-center-near-cedar-city; {'url': 'https://www.ksl.com/article/51507699/iron-county-planning-commission-approves-conditional-use-permit-for-antelope-data-campus', 'title': 'Iron County Planning Commission approves Antelope CUP'}; {'url': 'https://www.sltrib.com/news/2026/06/06/iron-county-planning-commission/', 'title': 'Iron County Planning Commission approves data center'}; {'url': 'https://www.datacenterdynamics.com/en/news/640-acre-data-center-could-be-built-in-iron-county-utah/', 'title': '640-acre data center could be built in Iron County, Utah'}

Objective: Sue over open meeting violations in OpenAI approval
Opposition Groups: Empowerment Congress of Doña Ana County; New Mexico Environmental Law Center
Summary: Two lawsuits filed against Doña Ana County, New Mexico, over 'Project Jupiter' — a 1,400-acre data center complex near Sunland Park with $165B in Industrial Revenue Bonds (by OpenAI). Empowerment Congress and NMELC allege Open Meetings Act violations and improper LEDA tax rebates. Project includes natural gas power plants and desalination plant. Could emit more greenhouse gases than Albuquerque and Las Cruces combined.

Objective: Allow SMR colocation with data centers in Arizona counties
Summary: In Arizona, HB 2795 would prohibit counties (<500K pop) from denying zoning approval for small modular reactors after federal design certification and site permitting. Allows SMR construction colocated with large industrial users (data centers) without ACC environmental compatibility certificate. Cleared House on party-line vote Feb 26, advancing to Senate. Gov. Hobbs vetoed similar bill in 2025. VERIFIED 2026-05-26: HB 2795 (industry-friendly SMR county-zoning preemption) failed Senate third reading 15-13-2 on April 8, 2026 and never reached the governor.

Objective: 6000-signature petition opposing data center in Hill County
Opposition Groups: Hill County Concerned Citizens
Summary: Nexus Data Centers filed plans for a 600-megawatt data center campus in Whitney, Hill County, with an initial $750 million building due by October 2026. Residents packed a town hall citing water use from Lake Whitney (a petition with 6,000 signatures), noise, and tax abatements. The opposition has become a key issue in the 2026 Republican primary for Texas Senate District 22.

Objective: Oppose Amazon data center in Falls Township
Opposition Groups: Falls Township Residents Against Data Centers
Summary: Amazon is building a 1-million-square-foot data center on 1,800 acres at the Keystone Trade Center in Falls Township, Bucks County, fast-tracked through Governor Shapiro's PA Permit Fast Track Program. Falls Township Residents Against Data Centers organized opposition, citing energy grid strain, increased electricity rates, and diesel generator impacts. A regional survey found 42% of SE Pennsylvania residents object to data centers vs 34% in support. The project was approved despite community concerns.

Objective: Adopt data center zoning standards in Bryan
Summary: Bryan, Texas, City Council proactively annexed 162 acres near the RELLIS campus and adopted a Planned Development zoning district allowing data centers with stricter-than-normal standards including increased setbacks, noise requirements, and screening specifications. No data center project has been formally proposed yet. The preemptive zoning measures represent a community win in establishing regulations before development pressure arrives.

Objective: Impose 12-month data centers moratorium in Oneonta (Eco-Yotta)
Opposition Groups: Oneonta Residents Against Data Centers
Summary: The Oneonta, New York, Town Board passed a 12-month moratorium on AI data centers in March 2026 after residents presented more than 700 signatures, prompted by Eco-Yotta Inc.'s plans to rezone 150 acres of farmland. This came weeks after the nearby Town of Dryden instituted an outright ban following its own 18-month moratorium. May 15, 2026: The Town of Oneonta (Otsego County) board unanimously approved a 12-month moratorium on AI data centers and crypto-mining facilities after a 700+ resident petition; the board signaled it may pursue a permanent ban. Company: Eco-Yotta (County Road 9).

Objective: Block 847,000 sq ft data center replacing White Clay Corporate Center in Newark
Opposition Groups: White Clay Creek Conservation Alliance
Summary: Verdantas/Shelbourne proposed an 847,000 sq ft data center complex on a 44-acre site at the White Clay Center Industrial Park off Ogletown Road in Newark, DE. The proposal would demolish seven existing buildings and replace them with a three-building data center campus. Site is owned by NY-based Shelbourne (8M sq ft commercial real estate portfolio). The location is outside Delaware's Coastal Zone and is in zoning that already allows data center projects. Critically, the New Castle County Council passed new data center regulations in March 2026 that explicitly do NOT apply to the White Clay project (or other in-pipeline proposals) — only to newly proposed projects. The project remains in early stages of approval.

Objective: Impose 24-month data centers moratorium in Grant County
Opposition Groups: Citizens Action Coalition of Indiana
Summary: Grant County Commissioners passed a 24-month moratorium on data centers on March 2, 2026. The Area Plan Commission began drafting ordinance language focusing on transparency, resource impacts, and local benefits, including questions about client disclosure and annual energy/water reporting. Opposition was led by Citizens Action Coalition of Indiana. The community successfully achieved its objective.

Objective: Rally against Hillwood data center in Joliet
Opposition Groups: Joliet Residents Against Data Centers
Summary: 795-acre data center (Hillwood/PowerHouse) on south Joliet farmland above depleting aquifer. Plan Commission approved 7-1 despite 4+ hours of opposition testimony. Hundreds packed City Hall. Would be largest data center in Illinois. Plan Commission approved 7-1 on March 5. At the March 17 City Council meeting, a marathon 6.5-hour public hearing forced a recess -- vote delayed to Thursday March 19 due to election day restrictions. Developers promise $100M community contribution, 7,000-10,000 construction jobs, and 700 permanent jobs. Would be the largest data center in Illinois at $20B, 1.8GW, 795 acres. Joliet City Council approved the $20B PowerHouse/Hillwood campus 8-1 on March 20 after a 6.5-hour public hearing. Only Councilmember Suzanna Ibarra voted no.

Issue Category: community_impact; contract_guarantees; design_standards; farmland; noise; zoning
Objective: Impose 55 dB noise limit on data centers in Lake County
Opposition Groups: Citizens Action Coalition; Hoosier Action
Summary: Lake County, Indiana, Council approved stringent data center regulations: only permitted in industrial zones with special approval, 200-ft setbacks from residential/agricultural, 55dB noise max, underground utilities, decommissioning plans, and annual firefighter training requirements. The project involves 160 acres. Opposition was led by Citizens Action Coalition and Hoosier Action. Key concerns driving this action include zoning concerns. The community successfully achieved its objective.

Objective: Six-bill legislative package addressing data center grid costs, tax breaks, and water use in Ohio
Opposition Groups: Ohio Consumers' Counsel
Summary: Sen. Kent Smith (D-Euclid) introduced a package of 6+ bills targeting data center impacts in Ohio: the Data Center Grid Cost Responsibility Act, a Tax Break Ban, the Powering Ohio Communities Act, the Local Development Protection Act, the Power Reliability and Fairness Act, and the Responsible Water Use Act which would cap daily water use at 5 million gallons. The Ohio Consumers' Counsel endorsed the package. The bills are pending committee action.

Objective: Impose 12-month data centers moratorium in Thomas County
Opposition Groups: Thomas County residents
Summary: Thomas County, Georgia, Board of Commissioners unanimously approved a 12-month moratorium on AI data center construction in January 2026. Commission Chair Mark NeSmith said the board was 'certainly not anti-data centers' but preferred to be proactive in protecting the community while gathering more information.

Objective: Petition to block Google data center in Irwin County
Opposition Groups: Suwannee Riverkeeper; WWALS Watershed Coalition; WWALS Watershed Coalition (Suwannee Riverkeeper)
Summary: Google proposed a 1GW data center on 546 acres near Irwinville in an agricultural zone. The Irwin County, Georgia, Planning Commission voted to recommend approval on Feb 26, 2026. However, the applicant withdrew the application on March 2, 2026 before the County Commission could vote, without prejudice to filing a similar application in the future. Opposition was led by Suwannee Riverkeeper/WWALS Watershed Coalition and concerned residents who raised alarms about water usage (1M+ gallons/day), impacts on the Alapaha River, noise, and farmland loss. Following the withdrawal, Irwin County began developing a data center ordinance, with a public hearing held March 17.

Issue Category: community_impact; contract_guarantees; design_standards; farmland; tax_incentive; water; zoning
Objective: Block rezoning of 648-acre data center site near Sunflower River in Clarksdale
Opposition Groups: Roundyard Neighborhood Residents
Summary: Clarksdale, Mississippi, Board of Commissioners voted on March 24, 2026, to rezone a 648-acre site between Sunflower River and Roundyard neighborhood from commercial/agricultural to light industrial for a potential data center. The rezoning came with three conditions: a one-time $5 million impact fee, the developer paying for all public utility improvements, and a 1,000-foot setback from residential areas. Earlier board meetings in January were contentious, with the board tabling the vote after a failed motion. No developer has yet filed an application. The site lies along U.S. 278 and Tallahatchie Street, and the vote is described as only 'the beginning of the conversation' about data centers in Clarksdale.

Objective: Reject data centers in agricultural preservation district and pursue environmental review in Nobles County
Opposition Groups: Nobles County Residents Against Data Centers
Summary: Nobles County (MN) Board of Commissioners voted 3-2 on April 21, 2026 to deny a zoning change that would have allowed Geronimo Power's proposed $4 billion, ~640-acre data center campus on farmland near Worthington — the change would have permitted data centers in agricultural preservation districts covering roughly 70-80% of the county. A packed room of farmers and residents applauded the decision; Geronimo CEO Blake Nixon said the company will seek alternative sites. The vote caps months of community opposition.

Objective: Challenge data center air permits in Faribault
Opposition Groups: Minnesota Center for Environmental Advocacy
Summary: The Minnesota Center for Environmental Advocacy (MCEA) filed an appeal against the City of Faribault over an 'inadequate' environmental review of Archer Datacenters' proposal on 87.6 acres. MCEA alleges the developer dropped estimated greenhouse gas emissions by 98% between draft and final stages without explanation, and failed to analyze cumulative impacts from the wave of nearby data center proposals. The appeal is active and pending resolution.

Objective: End by-right data center approvals in Prince William overlay zone
Opposition Groups: Coalition to Protect Prince William County
Summary: Prince William County, Virginia, Board of Supervisors voted to eliminate by-right approval for data centers in the Data Center Opportunity Zone Overlay District, requiring special use permits for all future facilities. Republican Supervisor Tom Gordy said the overlay 'has outlived its useful purpose' and suggested ending it outright. The same meeting controversially approved the Hornbaker Road data center outside the overlay in a 4-3 vote despite heated public opposition.

Objective: Require conditional use permits in Culpeper County
Opposition Groups: Piedmont Environmental Council
Summary: Culpeper County, Virginia, unanimously required conditional use permits for data centers on industrial land in September 2025, impacting 3,000+ acres across 140+ parcels that previously allowed by-right construction. Five projects in the Culpeper Technology Zone remain vested. The Piedmont Environmental Council and Coalition to Save Culpeper pushed for the stronger regulations.

Objective: Block GigaLand LLC's $3B, 600 MW data center on 202 acres near Remington
Opposition Groups: Citizens for Fauquier County; Protect Fauquier; Piedmont Environmental Council
Summary: GigaLand LLC resubmitted a scaled-down data center proposal near Remington after withdrawing the original in September 2025, reducing building space by 55% (from 2.2M to 998,662 sq ft) and cutting substation capacity from 1.2 GW to 600 MW, with a $3 billion estimated cost on 202 acres. On April 7, 2026, the Fauquier County Board of Supervisors adopted two resolutions targeting Dominion Energy: (1) opposing the Valley Link Joshua Falls to Yeat transmission line project, and (2) demanding Dominion bury transmission lines and reveal its high-load clients (data centers). The Planning Commission had previously recommended denial of the Morrisville substation expansion (4-1 vote).

Objective: Require quarterly water and energy reporting from data centers using 20+ MW in Iowa (HF 2690)
Summary: In Iowa, originally filed as HF 2447, Renumbered to HF 2690 after committee approval on Feb 23, 2026. Amendment H-8214 filed March 12. Key provisions: quarterly water/energy reporting, public disclosure within 30 days, separate utility rate class for data centers using 20+ MW. Sponsored by Rep. Cindy Golding (R-Cedar Rapids). First-of-its-kind legislation targeting water utilities specifically. VERIFIED 2026-05-26: HF 2261 (renumbered HF 2690; transparency/rate-class) did not reach the governor; Iowa sine die May 3, 2026.

Objective: Create separate utility rate class for data centers in New York
Summary: New York S8540, the Accountability for Computing and Data Centers (ACDC) Act, would require electric and gas corporations to establish an independent classification of service for large energy use facilities separate from other rate classes. The bill authorizes DPS to develop financial surety requirements and prevents data center costs from being blended into residential rate cases. The bill is pending in the Senate.

Objective: Require utility reporting of data center transmission impacts in NH
Summary: In New Hampshire, HB 1724, Sponsored by Rep. Wendy Thomas, Requires public utilities to submit annual reports detailing transmission costs, wholesale electricity market impacts, and generation capacity adequacy, with specific analysis of data center-related demand. Mandates public hearings and review by the state energy agency. Referred to the House Science, Technology and Energy Committee with a public hearing held February 10, 2026. VERIFIED 2026-05-26: HB 1724 (utility transparency/cost reporting incl. data-center demand analysis) died due to inactivity.

Objective: Appropriate $500K to study large-scale industrial water use by data centers in Wyoming
Summary: Wyoming HB0090 would have appropriated $500K to the State Engineer to study how large-scale industrial water use by data centers, carbon capture, and hydrogen projects impacts the state's water supply. The bill advanced 8-1 out of the House Agriculture Committee and received House Appropriations Committee approval on February 16, 2026, but did not pass the full chamber. The study was part of growing concern in Wyoming as data center developers eye the state for its cheap land and energy resources.

Objective: Redirect data center tax revenue to state fund
Summary: In Mississippi, HB 1635 was introduced to redirect 80% of local ad-valorem taxes collected above $1B from large projects (including data centers) into a new state infrastructure and economic development fund, with 20% to local government and schools. Madison County Economic Development Authority director Joey Deason objected to the measure, warning it would slash his county's projected $70M/year in new Amazon data center tax revenue to just $20M/year. The author did not bring the bill to committee vote.

Objective: Oppose Menlo Equities data center over water in Ahwatukee
Opposition Groups: Ahwatukee Foothills Residents Against Data Centers
Summary: Menlo Equities proposed a data center on 40.6 acres in the Ahwatukee, Arizona, Foothills area of Phoenix. The Ahwatukee Foothills Residents Against Data Centers organized opposition, citing concerns about water use in the desert, noise, and environmental impacts on the residential community. The project remains ongoing with the outcome pending.

Objective: 100-signature petition opposing Tract data center
Opposition Groups: Buckeye Residents Against Data Centers (ad hoc)
Summary: Tract is developing the 2,019-acre Buckeye Technology Park, the largest contiguous data center project in the Phoenix market, located near Palo Verde nuclear power plant and the I-10 corridor in Buckeye, AZ. Could see up to 20 million sq ft of data center space across as many as 40 individual data centers at full buildout. Tract is currently working with the local utility on plans to support up to 1.8 GW of capacity. $20 billion total investment over a 15-year buildout, currently in planning stage. Tract acquired the 2,069-acre site from Arizona Land Consulting for $136M+ (which had bought it for $40M in 2022).
